summary: I recall a hot horse running in the seventh race up at Arlington Park. One day, a guy who had been picking winners all afternoon wanted to bet five dollars on that horse, but the book wouldn't give him house odds because they thought he was using a system. Arlington Park was one of the tracks where the house odds mattered, and it was a place where the betting action was quite intense.

Chicago was the city where I saw the betting scene change over time. At one point, you couldn't get house odds in Chicago anymore because the books paid off on the mutuels. The whole atmosphere of betting and racing was very lively here, with joints like Happy's and places across the street bustling with people making bets and watching races. It was the backdrop for many stories about the ponies and the characters involved in the betting world.

There was an old white-haired woman in the book who always rolled Bull Durham cigarettes while she handicapped her horses. She was about sixty and had her own way of picking horses, naming her number one, two, and three horses during the race. Even though she never bet more than on those three, she was a keen observer and part of the daily scene at the book, quietly rolling her Bull Durham cigarettes as the races went on.

One night, we were all gathered in Charley's room, surrounded by racing papers and trying to figure out the next day's races. The place was so studious and packed with information that I joked it was like the Library of Congress. It was a way of saying how serious and intense we were about our horse racing research, with all the Racing Record, Form, Scratch Sheet, and turf selections spread out around us.
